constraints.special: Training forward pass - need f32 accumulation for numerical stability

analysis: f16 max value is 65504; accumulating K=4096 products causes overflow
Even without overflow, rounding errors accumulate quadratically with K
Training gradients become unstable: loss of significance in backprop
Mixed precision: f16 storage (2x memory bandwidth) + f32 compute (stable)
schedule_reasoning: f16 inputs: 2 bytes vs 4 for f32 → 2x memory bandwidth efficiency
f32 accumulator: 8 billion representable values vs 65536 for f16
Tensor core 16x8x16 MMA natively supports f16→f32 accumulation
f16 output with f32→f16 conversion in epilogue (round-to-nearest)
Standard recipe for transformer training: 'FP16 mixed precision'

constraints.special: Scientific computing - need full IEEE 754 double precision

analysis: f64: 8 bytes per element → 4x bandwidth vs f16
Without tensor cores: 1 FMA per thread per cycle
SM80 f64 tensor cores: 8x8x4 MMA = 256 FMAs per warp per cycle
Still 32x slower than f16 tensor ops, but 8x faster than scalar f64
schedule_reasoning: 64x64 tile: smaller than f16 due to 4x register pressure
8x8x4 DMMA: f64 tensor core instruction on Ampere
4 stages compensates for lower compute throughput
ColMajor layout: Fortran convention for scientific codes (LAPACK)
Used in: climate modeling, CFD, quantum chemistry, financial models
concept: Double precision f64 GEMM - scientific computing

constraints.special: Tall-skinny: M,N small but K very large - need K-parallel decomposition

analysis: Output is 128x128 = 16K elements, but K=32768 iterations per element
Standard tiling: 4 threadblocks for 108 SMs = 3.7% utilization
All compute is serialized along K within each threadblock
Split-K: partition K dimension across multiple threadblocks, reduce at end
schedule_reasoning: Split-K=32: partition K=32768 into 32 slices of K=1024 each
Grid becomes (2, 2, 32) = 128 threadblocks - much better SM utilization
Each threadblock computes partial sum for its K-slice
Final reduction via atomic add to output (or separate reduction kernel)
Trade-off: extra memory traffic for partial results vs parallelism gain
concept: Split-K GEMM - K-parallel decomposition for tall-skinny

constraints.special: Large matrices - L2 cache locality critical for performance

analysis: Row-major scheduling: blocks (0,0)→(0,1)→(0,2) access different B columns
By time block (1,0) runs, B[:,0:TileN] evicted from L2
8192x4096 B matrix = 64MB >> 40MB L2 on A100
Swizzle reorders blocks to maximize L2 reuse
schedule_reasoning: Swizzle<8>: 8x8 tile of threadblocks scheduled together in Z-order
8 consecutive blocks share B columns → 8x L2 reuse before eviction
Z-order (Morton code) ensures 2D locality: nearby blocks in grid are nearby in schedule
Can improve GEMM throughput by 10-20% on large matrices
concept: Threadblock swizzle - L2 cache locality optimization

constraints.special: MLP layer: Y = ReLU(X @ W + b) - fuse all ops into single kernel

analysis: GEMM output written to global memory, then read by bias kernel
4096x4096 temp matrix = 32MB written then read = 64MB traffic
Kernel launch overhead: ~5μs per launch, 3 launches = 15μs
Fused epilogue: GEMM result stays in registers, bias+ReLU applied before store
schedule_reasoning: LinearCombinationRelu epilogue: C = ReLU(alpha*A*B + beta*C + bias)
GEMM accumulator (f32) in registers → add bias → ReLU → convert to f16 → store
Zero intermediate memory traffic: temp never leaves registers
Single kernel launch: eliminates 2 launch overheads
Bias broadcast per_row: bias[j] added to all C[i,j] (column broadcast)
concept: Epilogue fusion - bias + activation in GEMM

constraints.special: Maximize MMA utilization via warp specialization

analysis: Each warp cycles between memory ops and compute ops
During memory load, MMA units idle; during MMA, memory pipe idle
Warp specialization: dedicate some warps to loads, others to compute
Producer warps prefetch data; consumer warps run MMA continuously
schedule_reasoning: 8 warps per threadblock: 2 producers + 6 consumers
Producer warps issue cp.async to prefetch next tiles
Consumer warps run MMA back-to-back with no memory stalls
Barrier synchronization between producer/consumer phases
Ampere cp.async enables asynchronous global→shared copy
Hopper (SM90) has hardware TMA; this is software approximation for SM80
